examples.title: Creating and Using Display List
examples.description: Complete example of creating a display list for a square and executing it
examples.explanation: Creates display list ID 1 with a red square. The list is compiled once but can be executed multiple times with different transformations applied before each call.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of vertices in list

examples.title: Hierarchical Robot Arm
examples.description: Demonstrates hierarchical modeling using matrix stack for a simple robot arm
examples.explanation: Uses glPushMatrix/glPopMatrix to maintain hierarchy. Each joint transformation is applied relative to its parent. Moving the shoulder moves the entire arm, while moving the hand only affects the hand.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is depth of hierarchy

examples.title: Transformation Examples
examples.explanation: Shows individual and combined transformations. Remember that in OpenGL, transformations are applied in reverse order of specification, so the code specifies scale→rotate→translate but the actual order of application is translate→rotate→scale.

examples.title: Multiple Display Lists with glCallLists
examples.description: Shows how to manage and call multiple display lists efficiently
examples.explanation: Demonstrates creating multiple display lists and using glCallLists to execute several in sequence. This is useful for batch rendering of multiple objects.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of lists called
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is total storage for all lists
